subject: Physics
solution:
The particle is moving in a horizontal circle, so it is accelerated towards the centre with magnitude v2r. The horizontal force on the particle is due to the spring and is given by kx, where x is the elongation and k is the spring constant.

kx=mv2r=mω2r=mω2l0+x

⇒k-mω2x=mω2l0

⇒x=mω2l0k-mω2

subject: Physics
solution:
Horizontal acceleration of the wedge (assuming the block and wedge move together horizontally):
$A = \dfrac{f}{M + m} = \dfrac{24}{10 + 2} = 2\text{ m/s}^2$

Working in the non-inertial frame of the wedge, the block experiences a pseudo force $mA$ directed opposite to the wedge's acceleration. The components along the incline are:
Component of gravity down the incline: $g\sin 37^\circ$
Component of pseudo force up the incline: $A\cos 37^\circ$

Net relative acceleration of the block down the incline:
$a_{\text{rel}} = g\sin 37^\circ - A\cos 37^\circ$

Using $\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$ and $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$:
$a_{\text{rel}} = 10(0.6) - 2(0.8) = 6 - 1.6 = 4.4\text{ m/s}^2$

Time taken to slide a distance of $8.8\text{ m}$ along the incline, starting from rest:
$s = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{\text{rel}}\, t^2$

$8.8 = \dfrac{1}{2}(4.4)\,t^2$

$8.8 = 2.2\,t^2$

$t^2 = 4 \Rightarrow t = 2\text{ s}$

Hence, the required time is $2\text{ s}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the length of the inclined plane be $s$.

For the smooth inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_1 = g \sin \theta$.

The time taken to slide down is $t_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{a_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{g \sin \theta}}$.

For the rough inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_2 = g \sin \theta - \mu_k g \cos \theta$.

The time taken to slide down is $t_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{a_2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{g (\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta)}}$.

Given that $t_2$ is $50\%$ more than $t_1$, we have $t_2 = t_1 + 0.5 t_1 = \dfrac{3}{2} t_1$.

Squaring both sides gives $t_2^2 = \dfrac{9}{4} t_1^2$.

Substituting the expressions for $t_1^2$ and $t_2^2$:

$\dfrac{2s}{g (\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta)} = \dfrac{9}{4} \left( \dfrac{2s}{g \sin \theta} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta} = \dfrac{9}{4 \sin \theta}$

$4 \sin \theta = 9 \sin \theta - 9 \mu_k \cos \theta$

$9 \mu_k \cos \theta = 5 \sin \theta$

$\mu_k = \dfrac{5}{9} \tan \theta$

Given $\theta = 45^\circ$, we have $\tan 45^\circ = 1$.

$\mu_k = \dfrac{5}{9} \times 1 = \dfrac{5}{9}$

Answer: $5/9$

subject: Physics
solution:
In both cases m1>m2. Therefore, m1 will accelerate downward and m2 will accelerate upward.

For m1,

m1g-T=m1a

For m2,

T-m2g=m2a

Adding the above equations we get,

a=m1-m2m1+m2g

⇒a1=m1-m2m1+m2g=2m2-m22m2+m2g=g3

 and

⇒a2=m1-m2m1+m2g=3m2-m23m2+m2g=g2

⇒a1a2=23

Change in momentum for each bullet fired is
$$
=\frac{40}{1000} \times 1200=48 \mathrm{~N}
$$
If a bullet fired exerts a force of $48 \mathrm{~N}$ on man's hand so $\rho$ man can exert maximum force of $144 \mathrm{~N}$, number of bullets that can be fired $=144 / 48=3$ bullets.

subject: Physics
solution:
From Newton's second law

$\frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{kt}$

Integrating both sides we get, $\int_{\mathrm{p}}^{3 \mathrm{p}} \mathrm{dp}=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{ktdt} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{p}]_{\mathrm{p}}^{3 \mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{k}\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{T}}$

$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathrm{kT}^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=2 \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{k}}}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The normal force $N$ provides the necessary centripetal force for the mass to move in a circle:

$N = m \omega^2 R$

For the mass to remain stuck to the wall without falling, the upward frictional force must balance the downward gravitational force:

$f \ge mg$

Since the maximum static friction is $f_{max} = \mu N$, we have:

$\mu N \ge mg$

$\mu (m \omega^2 R) \ge mg$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{g}{\omega^2 R}$

Substituting the given values $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $\omega = 5$ rad/s, and $R = 4$ m:

$\mu \ge \dfrac{10}{5^2 \times 4}$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{10}{100}$

$\mu \ge 0.1$

The minimum coefficient of friction is $0.1$.

Answer: $0.1$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, $\mathrm{m}_1=4 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_1=300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\mathrm{m}_2=0.8 \mathrm{~kg}=800 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_2=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ From law of conservation of momentum, $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2$ Let the velocity of combined system $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ then, $4 \times 300+800 \times 0=(800+4) \times v$ $\mathrm{v}=\frac{1200}{804}=1.49 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Now, $\mu=0.3$ (given)
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{a}=\mu \mathrm{g} \\
&\mathrm{a}=0.3 \times 10\left(\text { take } \mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right) \\
&=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
then, from $\mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as}$
$$
(1.49)^2=0+2 \times 3 \times s
$$

$\mathrm{s}=\frac{\left(1.49^2\right)}{6} ; \mathrm{s}=\frac{2.22}{6}=0.379 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
solution:
For motion without acceleration, net force = 0.
Component of weight along incline: $mg\sin(30°) = 5 \times 10 \times 0.5 = 25$ N (down).
Normal force: $N = mg\cos(30°) = 50 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 25\sqrt{3}$ N.
Friction force opposing downward motion:
$f = \mu N = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 25\sqrt{3} = 37.5$ N (up).
For equilibrium along the incline, applying force F down the incline:
$mg\sin\theta + F = f$, so $25 + F = 37.5$, giving $F = 12.5$ N.

solution:
Let, λ=mass per unit length of the chain and length of mass hanging through the table is x.

Normal force applied by the table on the chain will be, N=l-xλg.

Tension developed in the chain will balance the gravitational force acting in the downward direction, T=λxg.

Maximum limiting force possible for the part of the table, fmax=μN=μl-xλg

For equilibrium,

fmax=T,

⇒μl-xλg=λxg⇒12×6-x=x ⇒x=2 m
